title: Why some buses feel like a neighbourhood
summary: Describe a particular bus, tram, or minibus route that functions less like transport and more like a small community, and what that suggests about how cities work.
topic: A bus route that functions like a small community
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a specific bus, tram, or minibus route you have known where the regular passengers seemed to form a small community. Use at least one relative clause, the present perfect for ongoing observation, the first conditional for prediction, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: There is a route I used to take three mornings a week where the same passengers had been boarding at the same stops for so long that…
If you ride this route once, you will see only a bus, but if you ride it for a month, you will start to notice…
Although a bus is supposed to be the most anonymous space in a city, this one had quietly become…

hintVocab.meaningVi: vô danh, không ai biết là ai
hành trình đi làm hàng ngày
nhịp điệu của một tuyến đường

sampleText:
There is a marshrutka route in Tbilisi I used to take three mornings a week, climbing the hill from Vake to a school where I was teaching, and after about two months I realised the bus was not really a bus at all. It was a small standing community that re-formed every morning at roughly twenty past seven. The driver, who had worked this route for eleven years, kept a small notebook on his dashboard and would nod at certain passengers as if to confirm something. An older woman in a brown coat always sat in the second row and shared apricots with whichever child happened to climb on at her stop. A man in his sixties, who had clearly been a teacher decades earlier, gently corrected my Georgian whenever I attempted it. None of this had been arranged. It had grown the way a path grows across a field — through repetition.

Although a bus is supposed to be one of the most anonymous spaces in a modern city, this one had quietly become the opposite. I think part of the reason is that the route was small enough that the same fifteen or twenty people made up most of the morning load. We had all been boarding at the same stops for long enough that the geography of the bus had organised itself: the older woman in the second row, the carpenter near the back door, the two students who always took the seats nearest the front window. If a regular did not appear for several days, someone would mention it. Once, when the older woman was absent for almost two weeks, the driver asked her son about her at the next stop. He had remembered which stop her son boarded at, and the son was used to being asked.

What I have come to believe is that public transport can do something quietly important that planners almost never measure: it can give strangers a regular, low-pressure way to learn that the city contains other people. A neighbourhood does not only form between walls. It can form along a route. However, this only works at a certain scale. If the bus is too crowded, no one will recognise anyone. If it is too empty, no one will board. The route I rode in Tbilisi worked because it was both predictable and modest, and because the same driver had stayed long enough for his presence to be part of the structure. When that driver retires, I suspect the community will not survive the change — but for the years it has existed, it has done something for the city that no app has yet replaced.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: First conditional for likely outcomes
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If a regular did not appear for several days, someone would mention it' và 'If the bus is too crowded, no one will recognise anyone' minh hoạ điều kiện loại 1 (sự việc có thể xảy ra trong tương lai) và quá khứ thói quen với 'would'. So sánh: 'If + present, will + base' (loại 1, dự đoán); 'If + past, would + base' (thói quen quá khứ hoặc loại 2).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If a regular did not appear for several days, someone would mention it' (past habit with 'would') and 'If the bus is too crowded, no one will recognise anyone' (first conditional). Pattern: 'If + present, will + base' for likely future; 'If + past, would + base' for past habit or hypothetical.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for events before a past reference point
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We had all been boarding at the same stops for long enough that…' và 'the same driver had stayed long enough for…' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (had + past participle) để mô tả các tình huống bắt đầu trước thời điểm trong quá khứ và vẫn đang tiếp diễn lúc đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We had all been boarding at the same stops for long enough that…' and 'the same driver had stayed long enough for…' use the past perfect ('had + past participle') for situations that began before a past reference point and were still continuing then.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một tuyến marshrutka ở Tbilisi mà tôi thường đi ba buổi sáng mỗi tuần, leo lên đồi từ Vake đến một ngôi trường nơi tôi đang dạy, và sau khoảng hai tháng tôi nhận ra chiếc xe buýt không thực sự là một chiếc xe buýt. Đó là một cộng đồng nhỏ đứng cùng nhau, tái hợp mỗi sáng vào khoảng bảy giờ hai mươi. Người tài xế, người đã chạy tuyến này mười một năm, giữ một cuốn sổ nhỏ trên bảng điều khiển và thường gật đầu với một số hành khách như thể để xác nhận điều gì đó. Một bà cụ trong chiếc áo khoác nâu luôn ngồi ở hàng thứ hai và chia sẻ quả mơ với bất cứ đứa trẻ nào leo lên tại trạm của bà. Một người đàn ông ngoài sáu mươi, người rõ ràng đã từng là giáo viên nhiều thập kỷ trước, nhẹ nhàng chỉnh tiếng Gruzia của tôi mỗi khi tôi thử nói. Không điều gì trong số đó đã được sắp xếp. Nó đã phát triển theo cách một con đường mòn hình thành qua cánh đồng — qua sự lặp lại.

Mặc dù một chiếc xe buýt được cho là một trong những không gian vô danh nhất trong một thành phố hiện đại, chiếc xe này đã lặng lẽ trở thành điều ngược lại. Tôi nghĩ một phần lý do là tuyến đường đủ nhỏ để cùng mười lăm hay hai mươi người tạo nên phần lớn lượng khách buổi sáng. Tất cả chúng tôi đã lên xe ở những trạm giống nhau đủ lâu để địa lý của chiếc xe buýt đã tự sắp xếp: bà cụ ở hàng thứ hai, người thợ mộc gần cửa sau, hai sinh viên luôn ngồi gần cửa sổ phía trước. Nếu một khách quen không xuất hiện trong vài ngày, ai đó sẽ nói đến điều đó. Một lần, khi bà cụ vắng mặt gần hai tuần, người tài xế đã hỏi con trai bà về bà tại trạm tiếp theo. Ông ấy đã nhớ con trai bà lên xe ở trạm nào, và người con trai đã quen với việc được hỏi.

Điều tôi dần tin tưởng là giao thông công cộng có thể làm điều gì đó âm thầm quan trọng mà các nhà quy hoạch hầu như không bao giờ đo lường: nó có thể cho người lạ một cách thường xuyên, không áp lực để nhận ra rằng thành phố chứa đựng những con người khác. Một khu phố không chỉ hình thành giữa các bức tường. Nó có thể hình thành dọc theo một tuyến đường. Tuy nhiên, điều này chỉ vận hành ở một quy mô nhất định. Nếu chiếc xe buýt quá đông, không ai sẽ nhận ra ai. Nếu nó quá vắng, không ai lên xe. Tuyến tôi đi ở Tbilisi vận hành vì nó vừa có thể đoán trước vừa khiêm tốn, và bởi vì cùng một người tài xế đã ở lại đủ lâu để sự hiện diện của ông ấy là một phần của cấu trúc. Khi người tài xế đó về hưu, tôi nghi ngờ cộng đồng sẽ không tồn tại qua sự thay đổi — nhưng trong những năm nó đã tồn tại, nó đã làm được điều gì đó cho thành phố mà chưa có ứng dụng nào thay thế được.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the first conditional correctly?
options: If the bus is too crowded, no one will recognise anyone.
If the bus will be too crowded, no one will recognise anyone.
If the bus is too crowded, no one would recognise anyone.
If the bus is too crowded, no one recognise anyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: First conditional: 'if + present simple, will + base verb'. 'Will' does not appear in the if-clause; 'would' is for second/third conditionals.
mcQuestions.hint: 'If + present, will + base verb'; never 'if + will'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: The same driver has stayed long enough for his presence to be part of the structure.
The same driver had stayed long enough for his presence to be part of the structure.
The same driver had stay long enough for his presence to be part of the structure.
The same driver had been stay long enough for his presence to be part of the structure.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'. 'Stay' is regular, so the past participle is 'stayed'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect needs 'had' + past participle; 'stayed' is the past participle of 'stay'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly?
options: The driver, which had worked this route for eleven years, kept a small notebook.
The driver, who he had worked this route for eleven years, kept a small notebook.
The driver, who had worked this route for eleven years, kept a small notebook.
The driver, that had worked this route for eleven years, kept a small notebook.
mcQuestions.explanation: For people, use 'who'. In a non-defining relative clause set off by commas, 'that' is not used. Do not repeat the subject with 'he'.
mcQuestions.hint: Non-defining relative clauses use 'who' for people, never 'that' or repeated subject pronoun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'used to' for past habits correctly?
options: I used to taking this route three mornings a week.
I use to take this route three mornings a week.
I was used to take this route three mornings a week.
I used to take this route three mornings a week.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' expresses a discontinued past habit. 'Be used to + -ing' means 'be accustomed to' and has a different meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Used to + base verb' = past habit; 'be used to + -ing' = familiar with something.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'be used to + -ing' correctly?
options: The son was used to being asked.
The son was used to be asked.
The son used to being asked.
The son was used to asked.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be used to + -ing form' means to be accustomed to; here in the passive '-ing' form 'being asked'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Be used to' is followed by -ing; in the passive that becomes 'being + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly for concession?
options: Although a bus is supposed to be one of the most anonymous spaces, but this one had quietly become the opposite.
Although a bus is supposed to be one of the most anonymous spaces, this one had quietly become the opposite.
Although, a bus is supposed to be one of the most anonymous spaces this one had quietly become the opposite.
Although a bus is supposed to be one of the most anonymous spaces although this one had quietly become the opposite.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no repetition of 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: Never pair 'although' with 'but', and do not repeat the conjunction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I came to believe is that public transport can do something quietly important.
What I have come believing is that public transport can do something quietly important.
What I have come to believe is that public transport can do something quietly important.
What I have came to believe is that public transport can do something quietly important.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have come' (past participle of 'come' is 'come'). 'Came' is past simple; 'have came' is incorrect.

options: He would nod at certain passengers like to confirm something.
He would nod at certain passengers as if confirming to something.
He would nod at certain passengers as if for confirming something.
He would nod at certain passengers as if to confirm something.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As if + to-infinitive' is a compact way to suggest purpose-like imagined meaning; the other options use incorrect constructions after 'as if'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As if + to + base verb' expresses an imagined purpose or appearance.

options: She shared apricots with whichever child happened to climb on at her stop.
She shared apricots with whichever the child happened to climb on at her stop.
She shared apricots with whichever child was happen to climb on at her stop.
She shared apricots with whichever child happened climb on at her stop.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whichever + noun + verb' expresses 'any one of these that…'; 'happen to + base verb' is the correct collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whichever + noun' takes a regular subject-verb clause; 'happen to + base verb'.

options: We had all been boarding at the same stops enough long that the geography had organised itself.
We had all been boarding at the same stops for long enough that the geography had organised itself.
We had all been boarding at the same stops for enough long that the geography had organised itself.
We had all been boarding at the same stops for long enough what the geography had organised itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Long enough' (adjective + enough), and the result clause is introduced by 'that', not 'what'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Adjective + enough', not 'enough + adjective'; result clauses begin with 'that'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no app has yet replaced' correctly with present perfect?
options: It has done something for the city that no app yet has replaced.
It has done something for the city that no app has replaced yet still.
It has done something for the city that no app has yet replaced.
It has done something for the city that no app has yet replace.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Yet' in the present perfect typically goes between the auxiliary and the past participle, or at the end. 'No app has yet replaced' (auxiliary + 'yet' + past participle) is correct.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Yet' goes between the auxiliary 'has' and the past participle, or at the very end.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If shared bus or tram routes once created small civic communities, what is the equivalent — if anything — in a city where most people now travel by app-summoned car alone?
